title: Walking Calories Calculator 2026 — METs + Slope | Hacé Cuentas
description: Walking calories: how many calories do you burn walking? Calculation based on METs adjusted for speed, weight, and slope. With food equivalents.
intro: Walking is one of the most accessible forms of **calorie burn**: no equipment, no special location, no cost. The calories you burn depend on **3 factors**: your weight, your speed, and the terrain slope. This calculator applies the **Compendium of Physical Activities (Ainsworth 2011)** — the world reference for METs — and adjusts for uphills and downhills. It also shows the result in food equivalents for reference.
keyTakeaway: At 5 km/h (3.1 mph), a 70 kg (154 lb) person burns ~270 kcal/h walking. An hour of brisk walking ≈ 1 candy bar. 10,000 steps ≈ 350 kcal.
useCases: Estimate the calorie burn of your daily walk
Plan activity to create a calorie deficit for weight loss
Compare the energy cost of walking vs. jogging
See how many calories you burn uphill or climbing stairs
Justify an extra dessert portion after a walk

fields.label: Distance (km) — optional
fields.help: If you know it. Otherwise, enter time and speed.

fields.label: Time (minutes) — optional
fields.help: If you know it. Otherwise, enter distance and speed.

fields.label: Speed (km/h) — optional
fields.help: If you know it. Otherwise calculated from distance and time. Normal walking = 4–5 km/h.

fields.label: Slope (%)
fields.help: Positive = uphill, negative = downhill. 0 = flat terrain. For example, a hill trail can have 5–10%.

example.title: Step-by-step example
steps: **65 kg (143 lb) woman** walks **5 km in 60 minutes** (5 km/h) on flat terrain.
Steps = 5,000 m / 0.7 m per stride ≈ **7,143 steps**.

explanation:
## The Formula (based on METs)

```
Calories = MET × weight (kg) × time (h)
```

The **MET (Metabolic Equivalent)** is the energy cost of an activity relative to rest. **1 MET ≈ 1 kcal/kg/h** (basal consumption seated). A 4-MET activity requires 4x more energy than sitting.

## MET Table for Walking

| Speed | km/h | mph | MET | Intensity |
|-------|------|-----|-----|------------|
| Slow stroll | 2.5 | 1.6 | 2.0 | Very slow |
| Casual walk | 3.5 | 2.2 | 2.8 | Slow |
| Normal | 4.5 | 2.8 | 3.5 | Moderate |
| Vigorous | 5.2 | 3.2 | 4.3 | Brisk walking |
| Very vigorous | 6.0 | 3.7 | 5.0 | Fast march |
| Race walking | 6.8 | 4.2 | 7.0 | Nearly jogging |
| Slow run | 7.5+ | 4.7+ | 8.3 | Jogging |

## Slope Adjustment

Walking uphill significantly increases burn: approximately **+0.6 MET per 1% incline**. For example, walking at 5 km/h flat = 4.3 MET; on an 8% uphill it rises to 4.3 + (8 × 0.6) = **9.1 MET** (more than double).

On gentle downhills (up to -10%), burn drops slightly. On very steep downhills (> -10%), it drops more, though there's an eccentric braking component.

## Quick Reference (1 hour of walking, flat terrain)

| Weight (kg) | Slow (3 km/h) | Normal (5 km/h) | Fast (6 km/h) |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 50 | 100 kcal | 215 kcal | 250 kcal |
| 60 | 120 kcal | 258 kcal | 300 kcal |
| 70 | 140 kcal | 301 kcal | 350 kcal |
| 80 | 160 kcal | 344 kcal | 400 kcal |
| 90 | 180 kcal | 387 kcal | 450 kcal |
| 100 | 200 kcal | 430 kcal | 500 kcal |

## How Many Steps to Burn 100 kcal?

For a 70 kg (154 lb) person walking at normal speed:

- **2,500 steps** ≈ 100 kcal.
- **5,000 steps** ≈ 200 kcal.
- **10,000 steps** ≈ 350 – 400 kcal (depending on speed).

The famous goal of **10,000 daily steps** doesn't come from scientific evidence but from a **1965 Japanese marketing campaign** for the 'manpo-kei' pedometer ('10,000-step meter'). Modern studies suggest that **7,500 steps** already provide nearly equivalent cardiovascular benefits.

## Walking to Lose Weight: Does It Work?

Yes, with realistic numbers. To lose 1 kg of fat you need to create a **deficit of ~7,700 kcal**. Walking 1 h daily at normal pace (300 kcal) takes **~26 days** — as long as you don't compensate by eating more, which is very common. Walking is excellent as a complement to a nutritional plan, not as the only strategy.

Long-term, the big benefits of walking are **cardiovascular**: 30 min/day reduces all-cause mortality by ~30%.

faq.q: How many calories do I burn if I walk 1 km?
faq.a: A 70 kg (154 lb) person burns **~50–60 kcal per km** walking at normal speed. Simple rule: **calories per km ≈ weight × 0.7** (with weight in kg). On hills or fast walks the value rises. Distance matters more than speed for total burn.

faq.q: Is it better to walk fast or for longer?
faq.a: **For total calorie burn**, what matters most is **total time × intensity**. 1 h at 5 km/h ≈ 30 min at 8 km/h. For cardiovascular health improvement, **higher intensity** has extra benefits (improves VO2max, insulin sensitivity). But walking slowly for a long time is also very healthy.

faq.q: How do I know what speed I walk at?
faq.a: A **normal walk** is usually 4–5 km/h. To measure: 1) Use your phone or watch GPS. 2) Or time yourself: 1 km in 12 min = 5 km/h, in 10 min = 6 km/h. Talk test: if you can speak full sentences but not sing, **moderate** intensity (~5 km/h). If only short phrases, **vigorous** (~6+ km/h).

Apps like Apple Health, Google Fit, Strava, or Garmin are **reasonably reliable** (±10–20%). They over- or underestimate based on configured weight, terrain, sensors. Manual MET calculation (like this calculator) is similar. Use them as general reference, not exact measurement.

faq.q: Do I burn more climbing stairs?
faq.a: Yes — **much more**. Climbing stairs is ~8 MET (vs. 3.5 MET normal walking), or **2.3 times more calories per minute**. Descending is ~3.5 MET. That's why a couple of flights of stairs a day equals a short walk and is very effective if you're short on time.

Yes, especially **at a vigorous pace** (5+ km/h). It meets the WHO guidelines of 150 min/week of moderate activity. Improves cardiovascular capacity, lowers blood pressure, improves glucose, strengthens bones, and reduces dementia risk. The big advantage: very low impact, virtually no injuries.

faq.q: Does walking burn fat or carbs?
faq.a: **At low intensity (< 60% max HR)** the body uses proportionally more fat as fuel. **At high intensity** it uses more carbs. But what matters for weight loss is **total caloric balance**, not the fuel of the moment. A vigorous walk burns more total calories and more absolute fat than a slow walk.

faq.q: What if I walk with a weighted vest?
faq.a: Carrying weight increases calorie burn **~10% per 5 kg (11 lb) added** at normal speed. A 10 kg (22 lb) vest in a 60-min walk raises kcal from ~280 to ~340. However, heavy weights increase joint load: start with 5 kg (11 lb) and build up gradually.

sources.title: Ainsworth BE et al. — Compendium of Physical Activities (2011)
